danger_signals.visual_form: Low score display / medal position ranking
danger_signals.meaning: Player athlete underperforming relative to competitors

danger_signals.visual_form: Scoreboard updates showing world/Olympic records by computer athletes
danger_signals.meaning: Increased difficulty threat; opponent capability exceeds normal performance baseline
Higher tiers manifest as computer athletes breaking records and achieving superior results

safety_signals.visual_form: Position ranking (1st-12th place) with points allocation
safety_signals.meaning: Successful competition outcome; higher positions yield higher point values (24 for gold, declining by 1 point per position)
Low-stakes environment for player skill development without competitive pressure
Computer opponents perform mediocrely, ensuring accessible competition for learning players

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Selectable playable nation with unique anthem snippet
Selectable playable nation with unique anthem snippet
Specializes in archery; typically ranks top positions in this event
Specializes in swimming; typically dominates 200m freestyle swimming event
Selectable playable nation with unique anthem snippet
Host nation (Barcelona 1992); selectable playable nation with unique anthem snippet
Specializes in pole vault; typically dominates this event. Represents post-Soviet athlete collective
Specializes in sprinting events (100m, 200m); typically achieves top positions

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Scoreboard displays mediocre computer athlete performance across all events
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Computer-controlled athletes achieve lower times/scores; world and Olympic records remain unbroken

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Scoreboard shows improved computer athlete performance; some records may be challenged
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Moderate computer performance; increased likelihood of record-breaking at this tier

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Scoreboard regularly displays world and Olympic records broken by computer athletes
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Computer opponents perform at elite levels consistently; heavily luck-dependent outcomes; significant challenge to player victory

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Selectable event subset configuration interface visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can disable specific events; customized competition structure reduces full Olympic scope

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: All 8 events displayed in sequential progression
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Complete Olympic ceremony simulation; comprehensive athlete evaluation across all disciplines

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Computer athlete performance spikes in country-specialized events (Germany/archery, Italy/swimming, Unified Team/pole vault, USA/sprinting)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Computer-controlled athletes from specific nations consistently outperform in their specialty events

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Coca-Cola logo on zeppelin above scoreboard; branded jingle plays

environmental_storytelling.example: Each of 8 playable nations features unique audio signature during selection/introduction, establishing cultural identity and geopolitical representation
Computer athletes (e.g., J. Balen) have documented strengths/weaknesses and event-specific performance records, creating illusion of persistent athlete careers and rivalry narratives
Game design encodes geopolitical/historical athletic dominance: Germans excel in archery, Russians in pole vault, Americans in sprinting — reflecting real-world Olympic patterns
Point allocation system (gold=24 points, declining to 13th place=13 points) mirrors authentic Olympic medal table structure, reinforcing official competition legitimacy
Olympic difficulty tier unlocks record-breaking behavior, signifying transition from amateur (Club) → professional (National) → elite (Olympic) competitive strata
Spain featured as selectable nation in 1992 Barcelona Olympics; environmental choice reinforces historical event specificity

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Mediocre performance; no record-breaking; predictable average results across all events
Improved performance tier; occasional record-breaking; variable but competitive results
Elite performance; frequent world/Olympic record-breaking; consistently high scores; luck-dependent outcomes; significant threat to player victory

entity_behavioral_tells.entity: Nation-specialized athlete (e.g., German archer, Italian swimmer)
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Top ranking probability; record-breaking likelihood; visibly superior performance vs. general computer opposition
Average to below-average performance; no record-breaking tendency; vulnerability to skilled player
High difficulty and luck dependency; heavy reliance on button-mashing precision; computer opposition notably capable
Reduced/no button-mashing mechanic; skill-based control; player agency over outcome amplified; opportunity for high scores (9.0+) with practice

entity_behavioral_tells.description: Experienced player behavioral tell: capable of clearing 6.35m with consistent practice; demonstrates learnable skill progression

notes: Olympic Gold uses sports competition mechanics to manifest world-state primarily through difficulty-tier behavioral shifts and nation-specific event dominance patterns. The game encodes geopolitical athletic hierarchies visually through scoreboard performance and structurally through AI specialization. Sponsorship integration (Coca-Cola zeppelin in European version) represents commercialization as environmental worldbuilding element. The 1992 Barcelona setting anchors all nation selections to historical accuracy. Button-mashing vs. skill-based event variance creates mechanical storytelling: accessibility events (diving, archery) signal player agency; luck-dependent events (sprinting, hammer) signal systemic unpredictability at elite tiers.
